name: Eligibility Quiz
purpose: Determine whether the principal applicant appears eligible to proceed
Detect cases outside safe self-service scope
Route user to the correct path (solo, partnership, family)
Collect only information necessary at eligibility stage
user_stages: Stage A - Early explorer: heard about E-2, knows little, no business decided
Stage B - Researcher: knows the basics, considering options, no commitment
Stage C - Serious: has a business in mind, hasn't committed funds yet
Stage D - Ready: business selected, funds committed, ready to apply

questions.question: Which country are you a citizen of?
questions.display_note: The E-2 visa is available to citizens of countries that have a qualifying treaty with the United States. Start typing your country to see if you qualify.
questions.tooltip: This matters because the E-2 visa is only available to people who hold citizenship — not just residency — in a country that has a qualifying treaty with the United States. There are currently 82 qualifying countries.

special_routing.india: DO_NOT_PROCEED - Show EB-5 and citizenship-by-investment options
special_routing.russia: DO_NOT_PROCEED - Show EB-5 and alternative investor visa options

questions.question: Do you have a valid [nationality] passport?
options: It has expired but I am still a citizen
No — I do not have one
questions.tooltip: A valid passport is required for the consulate interview. It does not need to be valid right now — but it must be valid before your interview date.

questions.question: Where are you living right now?
options: In the United States — on a valid visa
In the United States — I am not sure of my status
In the United States — without valid status
questions.tooltip: Where you are right now affects where your visa interview will take place and which application process applies to you.

questions.question: Have you applied for an E-2 Treaty Investor visa before?
options: No — this is my first time
Yes — I was approved and I am renewing or reapplying
Yes — my application is still pending or under review
questions.tooltip: This helps us understand your history so we can prepare the right documents. A prior denial is not disqualifying — but it does require specific handling.

questions.question: Where are you in the investment process?
options: I have already committed or transferred funds to a U.S. business
I have a business in mind and I am getting ready to commit funds
I have the funds but I have not chosen a business yet
I am still figuring out the finances
I do not have funds available right now
questions.tooltip: The E-2 requires investment funds to be committed — not just available in a bank account. But you can start preparing your application long before that step.

questions.question: How much are you planning to invest?
questions.helper_text: There is no official minimum. Most successful applications involve $100,000 USD or more — but what matters most is whether your investment is substantial relative to the total cost of the business.
questions.tooltip: The law does not set a specific dollar minimum. But consulate officers assess whether your investment is 'substantial' relative to the cost of the business you are buying into. In practice, most approved applications involve $100,000 or more.

questions.question: Do you know where your investment money will come from?
options: Yes — savings, property equity, investments, or retirement accounts I can trace on paper
Yes — but parts of it may be harder to document
I have the money but I have not thought about the paper trail yet
I am still working out where the funds will come from
questions.tooltip: Documenting where your funds came from is one of the most scrutinized parts of an E-2 application. Every dollar needs a paper trail — from its origin to the U.S. business account.

questions.question: Are you planning to use any borrowed money as part of your investment?
options: No — all my funds are from my own savings or assets
Yes — a loan secured against my own property or personal assets
Yes — a loan that would be backed by the U.S. business itself
I am not sure how the loan would be structured
I have not thought about this yet

routing.personal_loan: proceed with note about personal-asset-secured loans qualifying

questions.tooltip: The E-2 requires that your investment is genuinely 'at risk' — meaning if the business fails, you personally lose the money. Loans secured only against the business itself don't meet this standard.

questions.question: How do you picture yourself running this business?
options: Hands on — I want to be actively involved every day
As a manager — I will oversee the business and hire people to run daily operations
With a partner — we will run it together as equal co-owners

routing.not_sure: proceed - note that this is fine at this stage

questions.tooltip: The E-2 visa is for investors who come to the U.S. to actively run their business — not passive investors. You do not need to work behind a counter every day, but you do need to be genuinely in charge.

questions.question: Will you own this business on your own or with someone else?
options: With more than one other person

routes.partnership: With one other person as an equal partner

routing.not_decided: proceed - note will ask again later

questions.tooltip: This affects how your application is structured. A two-investor 50/50 partnership is a recognized structure under E-2 law. Three or more equal partners creates a legal complexity that requires attorney guidance.

questions.question: Have you decided on a business yet?
options: Yes — I have a specific business or franchise in mind
I have a general idea but have not decided yet
I am completely open — I have not started looking

sub_question.show_if: Yes — I have a specific business or franchise in mind
sub_question.question: What kind of business is it?
options: A franchise — buying into an established brand
Buying an existing business from its owner
Starting a new business from scratch
A property rental or investment business

routing.general_direction: proceed with note that Module 2 will help find business
routing.completely_open: proceed - note you are in the right place

questions.tooltip: You do not need to have a business picked to use this app. But if you do have one in mind, we want to flag early if it raises any E-2 compatibility questions.

questions.question: Which U.S. state are you thinking of for your business?

state_metadata.notes: State-specific licensing and tax info auto-populates in checklist

routing.specific_state: proceed - store state for checklist generation
routing.not_decided: proceed with note that Module 2 will help decide

questions.tooltip: The state where your business is located affects your licensing requirements, tax picture, and application timeline.

questions.question: When are you hoping to submit your visa application?
options: I am in early planning — no specific timeline yet
questions.tooltip: This helps us calibrate which steps to highlight first. Someone submitting in 3 months needs a very different roadmap from someone planning 18 months out.

questions.question: Have you ever been refused a U.S. visa?
options: Yes — once, more than 5 years ago
Yes — once, in the last 5 years
questions.tooltip: U.S. visa refusals must be disclosed on the DS-160 application form — honesty is required. A prior refusal is not automatically disqualifying, but it does require specific handling.

questions.question: Have you ever been refused entry to the U.S., deported, or asked to leave?
questions.tooltip: Past immigration issues must be disclosed. An attorney can help you understand how they affect your E-2 application and what documentation or explanation is needed.

questions.question: Have you ever been arrested, charged, or convicted of a crime — in any country?
questions.helper_text: Include anything that was charged, even if it was later dropped or pardoned. The DS-160 requires full disclosure.
options: Yes — a minor matter, more than 10 years ago
Yes — a minor matter, in the last 10 years
questions.tooltip: The DS-160 asks about criminal history in all countries. Full disclosure is required — non-disclosure is treated as misrepresentation, which is a more serious issue than the original matter.

questions.question: Do you own property in [home country]?
options: Yes — and I plan to keep it
Yes — but I am thinking of selling before I move
No — I do not own property there
questions.tooltip: The E-2 is a nonimmigrant visa — you must demonstrate you intend to return home when your visa expires. Keeping your home or other property in your home country is one of the strongest signals of that intent.

questions.question: Will any family members — including extended family — remain in [home country] when you move to the U.S.?
questions.helper_text: Family ties to [home country] are one of the strongest indicators that you intend to return home when your visa expires. This includes parents, siblings, grandparents, adult children, or any close relatives staying behind.
options: Yes — immediate family will remain (spouse, children, parents)
Yes — extended family will remain (siblings, grandparents, other relatives)
Some family will stay, some will come with me
No — all close family is moving with me
I do not have close family in [home country]

questions.question: Will you keep your [home country] bank accounts and savings open after you move?
options: Yes — I plan to keep my accounts open
Probably — I have not decided yet
No — I plan to close everything and move it all
questions.tooltip: Maintaining financial accounts in your home country is a simple and effective way to demonstrate ties and nonimmigrant intent. Closing everything before your interview can raise questions.

questions.question: Will your spouse or any children be applying for E-2 dependent status alongside you?
questions.helper_text: E-2 dependents travel on the same visa category. Your spouse may also apply for U.S. work authorization once in the country.

routing.not_decided: show family section with optional framing

sub_question.question: Are you and your spouse legally married?
options: We are in a common-law or de facto relationship

questions.tooltip: Your spouse and unmarried children under 21 can join you in the U.S. on E-2 dependent status. Your spouse can also apply for work authorization once in the U.S.

questions.question: Is your partner a citizen of a country that qualifies for the E-2 visa?

Q0-09: With more than one other person

questions.helper_text: Your partner does not need to be from the same country as you — they just need to be a citizen of any E-2 treaty country.
options: Yes — they are a citizen of a treaty country
questions.tooltip: Your partner does not need to be from the same country as you — they just need to be a citizen of any E-2 treaty country.

questions.question: Will your partner be actively involved in running the business day to day?

Q0-09: With more than one other person

options: Yes — they will be hands-on in the business
No — they will be more of a silent partner

questions.question: Does everyone coming with you have a valid passport?
options: Yes — everyone has a valid passport
No — one or more passports are expired or missing

questions.question: How old are the children who will be coming with you?
options: One or more are 21 or older
One or more are close to turning 21 (within the next 2–3 years)
questions.tooltip: E-2 dependent status is available to unmarried children under 21. The day a child turns 21, their dependent status ends — this requires planning ahead if your child is close to that age.

questions.question: Has your spouse or any of your children ever had a U.S. visa refused, been denied entry, or had any criminal matters?
questions.tooltip: Each family member's application is reviewed independently. Issues with a dependent's background can affect their visa outcome — though not necessarily yours.

questions.question: Where should we send your eligibility result and your personalized next-step summary?

checkbox.label: Send me occasional updates and tips about the E-2 process
checkbox.note: You can unsubscribe at any time. We do not share your information with third parties without your consent.

questions.question: Before you continue, please confirm you understand the following.
questions.acknowledgment_text:
e2go is a visa preparation tool — not a law firm. Based on your answers, your situation has some complexity that may benefit from legal advice.

You are welcome to continue. We will flag these areas as you go and help you build the strongest possible application. But we want to be honest with you: a qualified immigration attorney may add real value to your case.

I understand this and want to continue.

hard_stops.PR-01: Citizen of non-treaty country — not eligible for E-2 visa
hard_stops.PR-02: In U.S. without valid immigration status — outside safe scope
hard_stops.PR-03: No funds available and no plan to obtain them
hard_stops.PR-04: Loan secured only by business assets — not qualifying E-2 capital
hard_stops.PR-05: Confirmed passive investor intent — E-2 requires active management
hard_stops.PR-06: Three or more equal partners — creates Negative Control issue
hard_stops.PR-07: Passive real estate investment — not an active qualifying business
hard_stops.PR-08: Cannabis-related business — conflicts with federal law

non_immigrant_intent_composite.description: After Q0-NI-01 through Q0-NI-03, count confirmed ties

investment_thresholds_usd.note: No statutory minimum. Thresholds are advisory based on observed consulate practice.

version_notes: v3.0 - Global expansion from Canada-only to 82 treaty countries
Added Q0-E2: Prior E-2 application history
Added Q0-STATE: Target U.S. state selector
Q0-01 now uses searchable_select with treaty country validation
Q0-10 now supports sub_question for business type follow-up
Currency input expanded to support 10 currencies
All Canada-specific references changed to home country
Stage-aware routing for investment and business questions
